original_hypothesis.title: Public pause/unpause callable by any PAUSER_ROLE member can be chained with immediate config changes to trigger liquidations or block exits
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Any address holding PAUSER_ROLE can pause the protocol, preventing user entrypoints (whenNotPaused) from executing. While paused, privileged roles remain active (docs indicate admin/bots still function). A PAUSER_ROLE holder could pause, then (if also admin or coordinated with admin) update oracle/CR configs and unpause, creating a narrow operational window where users cannot respond (withdraw/move collateral) but liquidations can execute immediately upon unpause based on new config/price feed.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is an operational workflow threat: role separation and incident playbooks matter. The code clearly allows pausing, and updateConfig is independent; the combination can be abused if roles are not properly separated or if a single entity controls multiple roles.
deployment_assumptions: PAUSER_ROLE is granted to more than a tightly controlled emergency actor
PAUSER_ROLE is held by an entity that can also influence DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE actions (same multisig or weak separation)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Deployment grants PAUSER_ROLE to `owner` in Size.initialize; additional grants are possible via GrantRole.s.sol. No on-chain role distribution evidence is provided.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pause/unpause are onlyRole(PAUSER_ROLE); updateConfig is onlyRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE); core user ops are whenNotPaused

preconditions: Attacker controls a PAUSER_ROLE key (or PAUSER_ROLE is broadly distributed)
Attacker can also execute or coordinate DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE updateConfig OR can rely on oracle updates outside user control
There exist borderline positions sensitive to config/oracle changes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Pause blocks user remediation actions but does not itself prevent admin configuration mutation; unpause restores liquidation surface immediately.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During incident/maintenance periods when pauses are used.

operational_questions: Are PAUSER_ROLE and DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE held by separate entities with independent controls?
Are there runbooks restricting config changes during pauses?
Are liquidations blocked during pause (they are whenNotPaused) and what happens immediately after unpause?
validation_requirements: Review production role assignments and multisig policies
Simulate pause -> config change -> unpause -> liquidation wave
Check monitoring/alerts for rapid pause/unpause sequences

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Pause protocol to freeze user operations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Change risk/oracle parameters (e.g., priceFeed, crLiquidation ratchet down) to make positions liquidatable
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Unpause and immediately liquidate targeted debt positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires privileged access; feasible if roles consolidated.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Partial (operational control abuse rather than code exploit)
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users unable to exit/adjust during pause; potential liquidation cascade at unpause.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Highest impact during volatile markets and legitimate pauses

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Pause/unpause frequency, role grants, rapid config changes around pauses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Same actor controlling pauser and admin actions

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
